Additive manufacturing of Titanium alloy for aerospace applications: Insights into the process, microstructure, and mechanical properties
=========================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Madhulika Srivastava a, V Jayakumar a, Yadu Udayan a, Sathishkumar M a, Muthu S M b, Preeti Gautam c, Akash Nag c

Show more

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apmt.2024.102481](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apmt.2024.102481 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2352940724004268&orderBeanReset=true)

Highlights
----------

*   •Insights into the additive manufacturing techniques for titanium alloys and their advantages over conventional manufacturing. 
*   •Titanium powder structure, size and distribution affect the microstructural evolution during AM. 
*   •Precise control over process parameters control microstructures for desired properties. 
*   •The property-microstructural relationship of Titanium alloys for aerospace applications is significantly influenced by the process parameters. 

Abstract
--------

The aerospace sector has been transformed by recent advances in titanium alloy additive manufacturing for aerospace components, which introduces novel manufacturing techniques and offers special benefits in design flexibility, shortened lead times, and cost-effectiveness. Titanium alloys have excellent mechanical properties in lightweight applications but are not feasible in terms of material efficiency during conventional manufacturing. The conventional machining of titanium alloys for aerospace applications faced significant challenges such as tool wear during machining, high buy-to-fly ratio making it economically not feasible and difficulty in fabricating complex geometries Metal additive manufacturing has appeared as a better candidate for manufacturing aircraft parts with a better buy-to-fly ratio and proper material efficiency in an economical way. The previous studies on additive manufacturing of titanium alloys have focussed on overcoming these limitations and enabling the efficient utilization of titanium alloys for complex aerospace components The present study aims to review the additive manufacturing to explore the intricate relationship of the process parameters with the microstructural changes and mechanical performance. It includes the effect of process parameters on the fatigue behaviour, tensile strength, residual stresses, corrosion resistance and microstructural evolution of additively manufactured components. The integration of the fourth industrial revolution (4IR) with additive manufacturing such as smart manufacturing, digital twin, and automated processes can enhance the efficiency and quality of the titanium alloy components. This implementation enables tailored design, microstructures, mechanical properties and rapid prototyping as per the requirements and specifications of the aerospace industry. Though additive-manufactured titanium alloy has made substantial advancements in the aerospace industry, further investigation is required to fully utilize its potential. The review highlights the potential to transform the aerospace sector by providing lightweight, high-performance components through advancements in process control and material performance and to fully utilise additively manufactured titanium alloy in aerospace applications.

Introduction
------------

Additive Manufacturing (AM) has reshaped the manufacturing sector by enabling complex and customised structures to be created directly from computer-generated design files. Because of its unique capacity to make lightweight, high-strength, and detailed components, AM has gained substantial interest in aircraft applications in recent years. The demand in the field of commercial aviation is rapidly increasing in the global market. It has been estimated that the requirement may increase to 28,000 new aircraft in the commercial market by 2031 while replacing 10,000 old aircraft Fields [1]. The aerospace applications (aircraft, space launches, missiles, satellites) need constant high-performance components for their products. The manufacturing in this sector is constrained by several interacting objectives, which allow the industry to grow technically and economically. As a fast-growing sector, they focus on lead time reduction for rapid production in order to meet the demand and to retain flexible designing of high-value components [2]. The former objective is interlinked with cost management and light-weighting. Lightweighting is a primary objective that drives the aerospace sector, which in turn affects cost management directly or indirectly. In order to achieve light-weighting, specific material considerations are obeyed that divert from economical production and on the other hand, light-weighting enhances performance that results in lower fuel consumption and more payload in terms of applications. High complexity and functional performance are the other objectives that determine the quality of products [2]. Optimum design and proper material selection counterbalance the above objectives. However, materials with such properties require precise machining to meet requirements efficiently. In such scenarios, industries are investing in technologies like additive manufacturing (AM), which can keep up with the fast growth and expenditure. AM is a promising technology in which layer-by-layer addition of material builds the object from computer design data [3]. Initially introduced as a method for rapid prototyping, AM advances to direct manufacturing of components in various fields, including aerospace, defence and biomedical [[4], [5], [6]], as it holds advantages over other manufacturing processes [7].

AM for aerospace applications offers definite benefits like: i) Design flexibility: AM enables engineers to create intricate geometries that were previously impractical to produce using conventional production methods. This design flexibility opens new possibilities for part optimisation for applications and system performance improvement. ii) Weight Reduction: Aerospace engineers are constantly looking for ways to reduce weight to improve fuel efficiency and overall aircraft performance. AM makes it possible to create lightweight lattice structures and topology-optimized components, lowering material consumption while maintaining mechanical integrity. iii) Customization and Rapid Prototyping: AM enables rapid prototyping and short iteration cycles, allowing for speedier design development and testing. Customization for unique user requirements, such as patient-specific implants or mission-specific components, becomes more practical. iv) Waste Reduction: Material removal methods in traditional production frequently generate large amounts of waste. AM, on the other hand, is an additive method that deposits material layer by layer, minimising waste and contributing to more sustainable manufacturing practices [11]. Unlike conventional manufacturing, this technology enables the fabrication of extremely customised objects with maximum material utilisation and minimum cost [8]. Other advantages include reduced lead time and the ability to fabricate components using monolithic, bimetallic, or multi-material compositions with varying properties [9,10].

Conventionally, materials such as aluminium, steel and nickel-based superalloys are used in aerospace applications. However, titanium alloys have increasingly replaced these alloys due to their superior performance in critical areas [12]. The key properties of titanium alloys to substitute aluminium, steel or nickel-based superalloys in aerospace applications are a) weight reduction (replacement for steels and Ni-based superalloys): Titanium alloys have a density (4.42 g/cm 3) almost half that of steel and offer outstanding strength, making them excellent for aircraft applications where weight reduction is crucial, b) temperature stability (replacement for Aluminium alloys, Nickel alloys and steels): It can be used in aircraft engines, and other high-temperature (1649 °C) applications because of its high melting point and resistance to extreme temperatures, c) corrosion resistance (replacement for Aluminium alloys and low-alloy steels): Titanium's superior corrosion resistance makes it ideal for aircraft components exposed to extreme weather conditions such as seawater and high humidity, d) limitation in space (replacement for Aluminium alloys and steels). These characteristics make titanium suitable for structural components, landing gears, engine parts, and other crucial areas where reliability and performance are crucial [13]. Some of the titanium alloys used in the aerospace application include Ti–5Al–5Mo–5V–3Cr-0.5Fe (Ti-5553), Ti–5Al–5Mo–5V–3Cr–1Zr (Ti-55,531), Ti–5Al–4Mo–4V–4Cr–3Zr (Ti-1300), Ti–5Al–3Mo–3V–2Cr–2Zr–1Nb–1Fe (Ti-5321), and Ti-6Al-4 V [13].Among these Ti-6Al-4 V is one of the most widely used alloys in aerospace applications due to its exceptional properties such as its high strength-to-weight ratio, corrosion resistance and temperature stability. It comprises of 90 % titanium, 6 % aluminium and 4 % vanadium which offers stability in mechanical properties and makes it suitable for manufacturing wing structures, springs, wing structure, engine parts and other aircraft components [14]. Despite these advantages, titanium alloy Ti-6Al-4 V has some constraints in machinability/weldability due to its low thermal conductivity (7.2 W/m k) which is responsible for the tool wear and high manufacturing cost [15]. These drawbacks in conventional machining made additive manufacturing an interesting alternative for the aerospace industry which enables the production of parts with complex structures and performance characteristics [16].

The objective of this review article is to give a detailed overview of the current state and future possibilities of titanium alloy additive manufacturing in aerospace applications. The study will investigate the different AM techniques used for titanium alloys, the mechanical properties of AM titanium components, design considerations for aerospace parts, qualification and challenges and emerging trends. Furthermore, the assessment will investigate the issues that AM titanium components encounter in aerospace, as well as prospective future applications.

**Challenges in conventional fabrication techniques**
-----------------------------------------------------

Conventionally, titanium alloy components in aerospace industries are manufactured using forging, casting or powder metallurgy, followed by post-processing such as machining or heat treatments [17]. These techniques are well-established in the aerospace industry primarily due to their reliability and consistency in manufacturing high-performance components [18]. These techniques allow precise control over dimensional accuracy and surface finish, making it suitable for large-scale production [17

Microstructures
---------------

The microstructural evolution during AM is influenced by the structure, size, and distribution of titanium alloy powder particles. The fine and uniformly distributed powders generate finer microstructures, whereas larger fragments can encourage coarser microstructures. The precision control of process parameters of laser or electron beam energy, scan speed, layer thickness, and cooling rates is required, as these characteristics have a significant effect on the microstructure and must be

Strategies to enhance the performance of AM fabricated Ti alloys and future prospective
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

AM of Titanium alloys is complex, as it involves interactions between numerous parameters such as laser power, scanning speed, layer thickness, and powder dispersion. Variations in these parameters can cause defects such as porosity, and inhomogeneous microstructures, which can have an impact on mechanical performance [132,133]. Conclusion
----------

Additive manufacturing has emerged as an innovative technology for the manufacturing of aerospace components due to its rising demand for producing lightweight, cost-efficient and highly functional components. This technology can fabricate intricate structures with a high degree of accuracy for both shape and dimensions while reducing waste and production time, thus making it significant for aerospace applications. *   ### [Improved dynamic impact behavior of TC4 alloy coated with Ti-SiC](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0167577X25007566)

2025, Materials Letters  Show abstract The dynamic impact resistance of the titanium alloys applied in dynamic load environment need to be improved. In this work, Ti-SiC coating was plasma sprayed on TC4 alloy and its dynamic impact performance was studied. The results showed that the multiple phases formed, such as Ti 5 Si 3, TiC, Ti 3 SiC 2, Si and residual SiC. Ti-SiC coating had the layer structure with good interface bonding and few pores. After Ti-SiC coating preparation, the peak dynamic compressive strength values increased by 13.6% (700 s−1) and 14.8% (1500 s−1), respectively. The coating anti-dynamic impact mechanism was that the impact energy were absorbed by reflecting impact stress waves using the multiple interfaces within the coating.

SUMMARY:

# The Role of Lightweight Metals in Metal AM

Industries are always on the lookout for materials that can make their products lighter, stronger, and more efficient. Lightweight metals are transforming the way we build everything from aerospace components to medical implants. These materials offer a high strength-to-weight ratio, corrosion resistance, and fuel efficiency, making them a game-changer in Metal Additive Manufacturing (AM).

### Why Do Lightweight Metals Matter?

Lightweight metals are key players in modern manufacturing because they allow engineers to reduce weight without compromising performance. Some of the most widely used include:

### How Metal AM is Changing the Game

Metal AM is revolutionizing how lightweight metals are used. Traditional methods often limit what designers can create, but 3D printing allows for complex geometries, lattice structures, and near-net-shape production, making it possible to push the boundaries of what’s achievable.

### Benefits of Using Lightweight Metals in Metal AM

### Key Considerations When Designing for Lightweight Metals in AM

To get the best performance from lightweight metals, engineers need to keep a few things in mind:

### The Future of Lightweight Metals in Metal AM

The demand for stronger, lighter, and more efficient components is only growing. With Metal AM, industries can continue to push the limits of what’s possible using lightweight metals. From [hypersonic](https://www.addmangroup.com/application/castheon-hypersonics/) aircraft to next-generation electric vehicles, these materials will play a crucial role in shaping the future.

At ADDMAN, we specialize in high-precision additive manufacturing, helping companies harness the full potential of lightweight metal components. Reach out to us today to see how we can help you create stronger, lighter, and more efficient products!

enriched_points.expanded_content: Additive manufacturing (AM) revolutionizes aerospace by enabling the creation of intricate, lightweight components directly from CAD models, enhancing design flexibility and reducing waste.
AM allows the fabrication of complex geometries, such as lattice structures, that improve performance and reduce weight, essential for modern aerospace applications requiring high efficiency.
Lightweight designs lead to improved fuel efficiency, increased payload capacity, and enhanced overall aircraft performance, addressing critical challenges in the aerospace industry.
